variables.content: The sequence consisting of (i) ab initio DFPT evaluation of long-wavelength phonons, Born charges, and dielectric response and computation of the long-wavelength Fröhlich vertex and its energy-resolved squared coupling density d(g^{2})/d\\omega to obtain the effective single-mode parameters \\tilde{g}_{\\mathrm{LO}}^{2} and \\hbar\\tilde{\\omega}_{\\mathrm{LO}}, and (ii) a Feynman-polaron calculation using the ab initio bare mass m_{0} and the Fröhlich parameter \\alpha computed from those inputs, reproduces the ARPES-measured hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e for orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ within experimental uncertainty; therefore this ab initio electron–phonon evaluation combined with a Feynman-polaron treatment is a viable predictive method to compute carrier effective masses in lead-halide perovskites when the Fröhlich mechanism dominates carrier dressing.

Polaron‑parameter calculations performed with variational polaron theory and material dielectric and effective‑mass inputs yield an estimated polaron radius r_p ≈ 36 Å for CsPbBr3; using the approximate mapping between polaron spatial extent and the carrier‑density threshold for overlap n_{\mathrm{overlap}}\sim 1/r_p^3 (modulo order‑unity packing factors), the larger r_p in CsPbBr3 reduces n_{\mathrm{overlap}} and thereby increases the propensity for spatial overlap of distinct polarons and for emitted optical phonons from different polarons to be reabsorbed, which enhances the density‑dependent hot‑phonon bottleneck observed experimentally in CsPbBr3.

Using ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) sampling of finite-temperature atomic configurations for bulk CsPbBr₃ at temperature T = 300 K in the canonical (NVT) ensemble with a Nosé–Hoover thermostat on a 3×3×3 pseudocubic supercell (135 atoms) and a 1 fs time step, and computing single-point electronic structures on a representative equilibrated AIMD snapshot with the Heyd–Scuseria–Ernzerhof hybrid functional including spin–orbit coupling (HSE–SOC, where HSE denotes the screened hybrid functional of Heyd, Scuseria, and Ernzerhof and SOC denotes spin–orbit coupling) using a hybrid mixing parameter α = 0.54 chosen so that the calculated band gap E_g = 2.37 eV matches the experimental room-temperature band gap, the direction-averaged hole effective mass computed for that room-temperature AIMD configuration is m_h = 0.265 m_0 (where m_0 is the free-electron mass). This computed m_h = 0.265 m_0 agrees with the angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurement of m_h = 0.26 ± 0.02 m_0 reported for CsPbBr₃, and this agreement is obtained without introducing any extra hole into the supercell (i.e., without polaron formation); therefore the ARPES-measured hole effective mass can be explained by thermal-lattice fluctuations alone under these simulated room-temperature, bulk-like conditions.

The momentum-resolved valence-band dispersion of single-crystalline orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ at room temperature was measured by angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (ARPES); here the measured quasiparticle energy as a function of the in-plane crystal momentum was fitted near the valence-band maximum (VBM) by the isotropic in-plane parabolic dispersion E(k)=E_{\\mathrm{VBM}}+\\hbar^{2}k^{2}/(2m_{\\mathrm{exp}}), where k is the magnitude of the two-dimensional in-plane wave vector (units Å^{-1}), \\hbar is the reduced Planck constant, and m_e is the free electron mass, and the energy zero was set at the VBM defined as the energy of the maximum of the energy-distribution curve (EDC) at the band top; this fit, performed on the upper valence band along the surface high-symmetry direction labeled \\bar{\\Gamma}-\\bar{M}, returns an experimental hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e.

Using continuum Fröhlich and Feynman variational formalisms to estimate large‑polaron parameters yields representative polaron radii for CsPbBr3 of electron ρ_e ≈ 26.7 Å and hole ρ_h ≈ 24.9 Å, and for representative organic perovskites larger electron polaron radii such as ρ_e ≈ 41.8 Å; for colloidal CsPbBr3 nanocrystals with physical size ≈15 nm and organic surface ligands, these continuum polaron radius estimates are taken as valid approximations for the screening length scale that controls dynamic Coulomb screening of interactions mediating carrier–LO‑phonon scattering, and smaller polaron radii correspond to reduced dynamic screening and therefore to stronger effective carrier–LO coupling in the nanocrystals under the stated experimental excitation conditions.

In room-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr3, the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum (VBM) located at the bulk Brillouin-zone R point was determined by fitting local quadratic dispersions and found to be m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0}, where m_{0} denotes the free-electron mass; this reported value is the average of four independent measurements on four different cleaved samples that produced individual fitted masses of 0.19\,m_{0}, 0.23\,m_{0}, 0.20\,m_{0}, and 0.19\,m_{0}. The identification of the VBM at the bulk R point was made by photon-energy-dependent ARPES mapping of the perpendicular crystal momentum k_{\perp}, and under the free-electron final-state mapping k_{\perp}=\sqrt{\tfrac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}}\bigl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}\theta + V_{0}\bigr)} with inner potential parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} (where \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} the photoelectron kinetic energy referenced to the vacuum level, and \theta the electron emission polar angle), the first R point in these measurements corresponds to photon energy h\nu=29\ \mathrm{eV}.

The modelling assumption is that in Ti$_2$O$_3$ the bare conduction-band (electron) effective mass $m_{\mathrm{e}}$ exceeds the bare valence-band (hole) effective mass $m_{\mathrm{h}}$ ($m_{\mathrm{e}}>m_{\mathrm{h}}$) and that lattice polarization associated with small-polaron formation increases the electron effective mass by a much larger multiplicative factor than it increases the hole effective mass, so that after polaron formation electrons become very heavy, non-degenerate polarons while holes remain relatively light and mobile.

For the photoexcited carriers in $CsPbBr_{3}$ nanocrystal thin films, the carrier effective mass renormalization due to polaron formation is characterized by a polaron mass $m_{p}$ equal to $3.5$ times the bare band effective mass $m^{*}$, with the bare mass taken as $m^{*}=0.21\,m_{e}$ where $m_{e}$ is the free-electron rest mass ($m_{e}=9.10938356\times10^{-31}\,$kg); explicitly, $m_{p}=3.5\cdot 0.21\cdot m_{e}$. This multiplicative factor is adopted from prior theoretical and experimental studies of related lead-halide perovskites and is assumed to be applicable under the present sample morphology, temperature, and excitation conditions.
      See cited literature providing polaron mass estimates used by the authors.

Introducing one extra hole (removing one electron) into an equilibrated 3×3×3 pseudocubic AIMD supercell (135 atoms) of bulk CsPbBr₃ sampled at T = 300 K and recomputing the electronic structure with HSE–SOC using α = 0.54 produces no localized in-gap electronic states and causes no substantial change in the unfolded valence-band dispersions relative to the corresponding polaron-free AIMD calculation; the absence of such localized gap states or strongly localized valence-band perturbations in these finite-supercell HSE–SOC calculations indicates that thermally stabilized small-hole polarons are not observed under these simulated bulk-like room-temperature conditions.

When holes interact with lattice vibrations via short-range Holstein coupling in the presence of short-range antiferromagnetic (AFM) correlations, the electron-phonon interaction (EPI) is amplified by constructive interplay with electron-electron interactions (EEI) associated with those AFM correlations, leading to formation of electron-lattice polarons whose effective coupling $\lambda(x)$ decreases with increasing hole doping $x$; comparison of computed single-hole spectra with ARPES and optical data implies that $\lambda(x)$ is larger at low dopings and decreases toward optimal doping, with the system approaching a self-trapping crossover between extended (large-polaron) and localized (small-polaron) behavior near optimal doping (the calculations reported lie below the model critical coupling $\lambda_c\approx0.6$), and with coexistence of extended and localized polaron character appearing in a momentum-dependent fashion in the crossover region.
